author: @derelictarchive
text:
The roller is traveling in a constrained circle. If the swash plate is a flat 0 degrees, then the roller is free to travel without the swash plate reacting and there is no output rotation. If the swash plate were somehow set to 90 degrees then it must be pushed one for one by the roller. I'll call this tidally locked. These extremes are easy to visualize. 

At intermediate angles you get output rotation between 0 and 1 relative to the input, but why? Consider the swash plate at 45 degrees or so. Before rotation begins the roller is well mated to the surface. The plane of the plate is parallel to the tangent line of the roller's circle of travel. When you rotate the roller it attempts to follow its constrained circle I mentioned before, but the swash plate extends before it in a flat plane which blocks the roller's travel. The roller must either be stopped or push that plane away from it to continue its own curved path. That's also not too hard to picture, I think. But why must the swash plate rotate instead of tilt out of the way? 

If you were to return the swash plate back to 0 deg, you could then tilt it again back to 45 deg in a different spot without rotating it at all. But we can't do that obviously, the tilt is constant whenever we aren't changing the 'gear' ratio. So, does this matter? After puzzling on it I think yes. If the swash plate is tilted then you cannot rotate the tilted spot under the roller without also rotating the plate itself. But the less tilt you have the less plate rotation is necessary. And so by changing the tilt you vary the output rotation as a consequence of geometry which I'm too rusty to describe mathematically. But not friction.

author: @tomp7141
text:
If you start an upsidedown dinner plate "wobbling" on a table, the plate will nutate and rotate.  The greater the angle between the plate's axis and vertical, the fewer nutations required per each rotation.  This is because the edge of the plate has a fixed circumferance but it rolls along a circular path on the table having a smaller circumferance.  Nutation and rotation both stop when the plate goes flat.
So, if you could output the rotation of a nutating disk (using a universal joint) AND control the angle of nutation of that disk while making it nutate (with a roller), then your output/input ratio could be varied from zero to some positive value.
Friction would still be required between the edge of the nutating disk and the surface it rolls on.

author: @aurigo_tech
text: One should imagine a crank on a shaft at 90°, where one could apply maximum torque and the maximum size circle the crankhandle would take. Now let that crank be at an angle, lets say 45° to the shaft. Then the torque one can apply will be lower but also the circle the crankhandle will take will be smaller. I understand this as a version of this concept.

author: @talyrath
text:
The friction is totally there, it's just not where you're pointing. 

The roller causes the swash plate to *nutate* on the lower sloped disc.  Imagine spinning a coin on a table.  As it slows down, it stops spinning and starts wobbling round and round on the edge touching the table, I.e. nutating.  

As the coin loses energy, the wobble on the rim gets faster and faster until it finally clicks down flat on the table and stops. BUT, if you look at the design on the coin, the design rotates slower and slower as the wobble gets faster and faster. THAT'S the mechanism of this CVT and it's also where the friction lives. The plate exerts a tangential force on the base from nutation and friction provides the equal/opposite force that causes *rotation*. 

If there isn't any friction between the swash plate and the base, the plate doesn't rotate.   To use Robert's analogy, the friction is what is keeping the lever from sliding off the fulcrum.   

And in this case, the friction LIMITS THE TORQUE that the CVT can provide. If you exceed the static friction between the swash plate and the angled base, they'll just skid against each other like a car spinning it's wheels on ice.

author: @thecellburner
text: basically this is a variable thread screw, if it gets more inclined the thread gets corser. Finer threads improve the strength of the power transmission but are slower due to the distance traveled. i get the advantage compared to a belt cvt because it has a lot less friction but i'm not sure if the fulcrum would last long considering it takes all the load. Instead of a triangle holding the load we'd gradually have a rounder surface reducing the " thread delta of the screw" . i don't know if fine tuning the hardness of the materials between the plate and the fulcrum could resolve this problem. i understand why you like cvts so much, in a quest for efficiency, the transmission of power is very important. thanks to you  i just understood how the pilot of an helicopter can vary the pitch of the blades with a swash plate when i looked for it...

author: @Alan_Hans__
text: I was 1 of the many commenters. I could/can see how it works and it works beautifully when driving from input to output. My main concern is that if you grabbed the output shaft it might be possible to drive it from the output end. What stops it is the pressure of the cam onto the inclined plate. The cam is going to be essentially frictionless so it then comes down to the friction of what is close to a point contact between the wobble plate and the inclined plate. I wish I had a 3D printer to print this up as it looks like it's as close to a frictionless CVT drive as exists. My only concerns is the lack of friction between 2 parts could cause a problem. About the simplest form of a failure mode would be if it was used with engine braking and the output is wanting to travel faster than the input. I'd truly love to be proven wrong as this would be the discovery of the unicorn of CVTs.

author: @mfree80286
text:
A super-simplified model might be easier to explain, one axle with a two part disc, a regular platter and hub made of TPU as a flexure (thick enough to want to resist tilting). The other axle would just have a solid hub with a "stylus" rod made with a rounded end that could be greased or a ball of felt glued to the end. Mounted linearly, pushing the axles together provides both engagement and deflection for the platter, which resists due to the hub's natural springiness. 
Would it transfer much torque? No. Would it be far easier to see how the mechanism works, stripped of everything that could be serving as a red herring? Yes.

author: @RyannMade
text:
If you could remove friction entirely then the roller would slide instead of roll and the plate would slide instead of roll.

Like if you had a ball on a surface. Without friction the ball slides instead of rolls.

You should be able to test this by back driving or observing what happens under heavy load. Any slippage is from the coefficient of friction between the plate and the cone being overcome.

author: @donvito1973
text: The swash plate is free to tilt in any direction.. As the roller goes around, pressing on your lever, tilting the swash plate in each successive direction, the swash plate wouldn't rotate, it would just tilt in a different direction.. What happens in practice, is that the difference in circumference of the swash plate and the circle it inscribes on the cone, through friction, causes the swash plate to rotate like an euler disc or a spinning coin. Without friction between the swash plate and the cone, the swashplate would be back to just tilting.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't think that's right mate because it doesn't just tilt - it tilts in sequence which is what causes the rotation - but we could be splitting hairs

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I just removed the cone and tried it - still works fine - so, I am really sure it has nothing to do with the cone

replies.author: @donvito1973
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering If we go back to your thought of pressing the swash plate at 45 degree increments, ignoring the rotation of the input shaft, just think of it like a lever like you say.. between the two points, the swash plate will roll around it's circumference like an euler disc... if the friction of the roller pressing the swash plate was greater than the friction of the edge of the swash plate rolling around the cone, then the disc would just tilt to the new direction, with no rotational movement at all.. it relies on the edge friction, and the lack of friction at the roller.
